title: Flexible bronchoscopy in mechanically ventilated critically ill patients: practical considerations and clinical applications – a narrative review
one_line_summary: Flexible bronchoscopy (FB) is a critical ICU tool. This review outlines practical, technical, and safety considerations for FB in mechanically ventilated patients, detailing scope selection, sedation, ventilator adjustments, BAL, and advanced interventions like cryoextraction.
key_pearls: Ensure ETT size is at least 1.5-2.0 mm larger than the bronchoscope's outer diameter, and use water-soluble lubricants only.
Pre-procedure, increase FiO2 to 1.0 and set ventilator Pmax alarm limit to 40 cmH2O.
Perform FB interventions in short, sequential cycles, limiting continuous suctioning to a few seconds.
If using PC ventilation, reduce inspiratory pressure to baseline *before* withdrawing the bronchoscope to prevent barotrauma.
Post-procedure, restore ventilator settings, reassess sedation, and immediately flush the scope's working channel with >250 mL 0.9% NaCl.

sections.heading: Selection of Bronchoscopes
sections.content: Flexible Optic Bronchoscope (FOB): Direct visualization via eyepiece. Requires external light source or battery-powered LED. Sizes range from 2.0 to 6.2 mm outer diameter. Working channels of at least 2.0 mm are recommended to accommodate therapeutic accessories and maintain suction capacity. - Fiberoptic Videobronchoscope (FVB): Indirect visualization via a video chip displaying on a monitor. Requires a separate mobile tower (demanding more bedside space). Offers higher resolution, larger images, and allows the operator to maintain a safe physical distance. - Single-Use Flexible Bronchoscope (SUFB): Sterile, eliminating cross-contamination and reprocessing requirements. Highly useful for out-of-hours procedures. While individual unit costs may be higher, systematic reviews suggest cost-effectiveness when factoring in the treatment of nosocomial infections. Current evidence suggests limiting SUFB use to bronchoscopic intubations, simple therapeutic aspirations, BAL, and low-risk percutaneous tracheostomies.
section_pearls: Working channels of at least 2.0 mm are recommended for FOBs to accommodate accessories and maintain suction.
Single-Use Flexible Bronchoscopes (SUFB) are cost-effective for out-of-hours procedures and specific low-risk interventions.

sections.heading: Indications for FB in the ICU
sections.content: Diagnostic: - Respiratory Infections (including VAP): Targeted sampling to identify pathogens, reduce mortality, and guide antibiotic de-escalation. - Respiratory Bleeding: Identifying the source/location of hemorrhage, clearing clots, and guiding embolization. - Airway Injuries: Direct assessment of thermal, chemical, or mechanical trauma. - ETT Management: Verifying correct placement and diagnosing long-term intubation complications. - Therapeutic: - Lobar Collapse: Targeted suctioning and lavage of mucus plugs refractory to standard chest physiotherapy. - Fiberoptic Intubation: Facilitating difficult airways (e.g., cervical spine instability, facial trauma). - Percutaneous Tracheostomy: Real-time visualization to ensure midline needle puncture, avoid posterior tracheal wall damage, and confirm tube position.
section_pearls: FB is crucial for targeted sampling in respiratory infections and identifying sources of respiratory bleeding.
Therapeutic FB can resolve lobar collapse and facilitate difficult intubations or percutaneous tracheostomies.

sections.heading: Complications: Fever, Infection, and Reprocessing
sections.content: Bacteremia & Fever: Transient bacteremia occurs in approximately 5% of patients, while post-bronchoscopy fever occurs in up to 50% due to cytokine release triggered by lavage, lidocaine instillation, or mechanical trauma. Post-bronchoscopy pneumonia occurs in up to 6% of patients. - VAP & Biofilms: FB is an independent risk factor for ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP). The insertion of the scope can introduce biofilm-enclosed bacteria from the ETT into the lower respiratory tract. Reprocessing reusable scopes requires at least High-Level Disinfection (HLD). Damaged or scratched internal channels can harbor persistent biofilms, leading to outbreaks of multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) like Pseudomonas aeruginosa and carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae. - Storage & Aerosols: Scopes must be stored hanging upright without valves in spacious, ventilated cabinets to prevent moisture buildup. Because FB is an aerosol-generating procedure, healthcare workers should wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE), including N95 respirators, gowns, gloves, and eye protection.
section_pearls: FB is an independent VAP risk factor due to potential biofilm introduction from the ETT; proper reprocessing (HLD) and storage are critical.
FB is an aerosol-generating procedure, requiring N95 respirators and full PPE for healthcare workers.

sections.heading: Procedural Sedation & Topical Anesthesia
sections.content: Sedation: Deepening of baseline ICU sedation is often required to suppress coughing and patient movement. Continuous infusions of propofol, midazolam, dexmedetomidine, or ketamine are titrated. Adding an opioid improves cough suppression, reduces lidocaine requirements, and enhances tolerance. Neuromuscular blocking agents may be used to facilitate FB in hemodynamically unstable patients where deep sedation is desired. - Topical Anesthesia: 1% lidocaine is as effective as 2% lidocaine for "spray-as-you-go" delivery via the working channel and is preferred to minimize cumulative dosing. - Intravenous Lidocaine: Administered as a single bolus of 0.5 to 2 mg/kg to suppress the cough reflex, cardiovascular pressor response, and prevent spikes in intracranial pressure (ICP) or intraocular pressure. Indicated in patients with traumatic brain injury, heart failure, or severe COPD. Cumulative dosing must be carefully monitored, especially in patients with renal impairment, hepatic dysfunction, or congestive heart failure.
section_pearls: Deep sedation with propofol, midazolam, dexmedetomidine, or ketamine, often with an opioid, is needed for FB.
1% topical lidocaine is preferred over 2% to minimize cumulative dosing; IV lidocaine (0.5-2 mg/kg) can suppress cough and pressor responses, but monitor cumulative dose in at-risk patients.

sections.heading: Lubricants
sections.content: Recommendations: Water-soluble lubricants are mandatory to facilitate passage through ETT connectors and prevent damage to the scope's distal rubber sheath. - Contraindications: Petroleum-based products (e.g., paraffin, petroleum jelly) and non-medical silicone must be avoided. They degrade the scope's rubber sheath, are highly resistant to reprocessing (harboring biofilms), and can cause acute or chronic lipoid pneumonia (including ARDS) if introduced into the lungs. Lidocaine gel is widely used but can dry out during long procedures. Medical-grade silicone spray is a safe, effective alternative.
section_pearls: Only water-soluble lubricants or medical-grade silicone spray should be used for FB.
Avoid petroleum-based products due to scope damage, biofilm risk, and potential lipoid pneumonia.

sections.heading: Endotracheal Tube (ETT) Interactions & Ventilation Modes
sections.content: Airway Resistance & PEEP: Inserting a 5-6 mm bronchoscope into an ETT significantly reduces the cross-sectional area (occupying 40% of a 9 mm ETT and 66% of a 7 mm ETT). This dramatically increases airway resistance, peak tracheal pressures (ranging from 18 to 60 cmH2O), and intrinsic PEEP (which can exceed 35 cmH2O). This hyperinflation can cause barotrauma, pneumothorax, and hemodynamic collapse due to reduced venous return. - Sizing Rule: Maintain a difference of at least 1.5 to 2 mm between the internal diameter of the ETT/tracheostomy tube and the external diameter of the bronchoscope (or use an ETT of at least 8 mm). - Ventilation Adjustments: Volume-controlled (VC) modes are generally preferred by experts. To prevent hypoventilation when resistance rises, the peak pressure limit (Pmax) must be temporarily increased (e.g., to 40 cmH2O), and low tidal volume/minute volume alarms must be adjusted. - PC Mode Caution: If pressure-controlled (PC) ventilation is used, the inspiratory pressure must be titrated upward to maintain adequate tidal volumes during the procedure, but it **must** be lowered back to baseline *before* the bronchoscope is withdrawn to prevent massive hyperinflation and barotrauma. - Procedure Execution: Perform FB in short, sequential cycles with suction intervals limited to a few seconds to prevent derecruitment and excessive intrinsic PEEP. - Apnea-Intermittent Technique: Proposed during the COVID-19 pandemic to limit aerosolization and prevent uncontrolled airway pressures (e.g., in post-lung resection patients). The patient is pre-oxygenated, disconnected from the ventilator, and the procedure is performed during apnea. If SaO2 drops below 90%, the scope is removed, and the patient is reconnected and re-oxygenated. This technique is not validated for routine use.
section_pearls: Bronchoscope insertion significantly increases airway resistance and intrinsic PEEP, risking barotrauma; maintain at least a 1.5-2 mm difference between ETT ID and bronchoscope OD (or use ETT ≥ 8 mm).
In VC mode, increase Pmax limit to 40 cmH2O; in PC mode, titrate inspiratory pressure up, but lower to baseline *before* withdrawal.
Perform FB in short cycles with brief suction intervals to prevent derecruitment and excessive intrinsic PEEP.

sections.heading: Bronchoalveolar Lavage (BAL) Protocol
sections.content: Physiological Impact: BAL can cause transient but significant decreases in lung volume and compliance. A decrease in the PaO2/FiO2 ratio of more than 25% occurs in up to 30% of patients, and hemodynamic instability occurs in 20% within 24 hours post-procedure. - Technique: 1. Perform thorough ETT suctioning prior to scope insertion. 2. Avoid suctioning or injecting lidocaine through the working channel during insertion to prevent sample contamination. 3. Wedge the scope tip in the target subsegment. 4. Instill an initial 20 mL aliquot of sterile saline to clear residual upper airway secretions, aspirate, and **discard** this sample (do not use for quantitative culture). 5. Instill subsequent aliquots of 20 to 60 mL (total volume of 120 to 140 mL) and gently aspirate. Avoid excessive volumes, as a greater than 40% increase in lavage volume is associated with increased VAP risk and sample dilution.
section_pearls: BAL can cause transient decreases in lung volume/compliance and PaO2/FiO2 ratio (>25% in 30% of patients).
Discard the initial 20 mL BAL aliquot to avoid upper airway contamination; use total lavage volumes of 120-140 mL (20-60 mL aliquots).

sections.heading: Clot and Mucus Plug Removal
sections.content: Standard Methods: Lavage, suction, biopsy forceps, basket extraction, or Fogarty balloon catheter dislodgement. Local instillation of thrombolytic agents (e.g., streptokinase) can be used for adherent clots but carries a risk of rebleeding. - Cryoextraction: A highly effective bedside technique (92-100% success rate). A cryoprobe is passed through the working channel, and a coolant (nitrous oxide or carbon dioxide) is delivered to freeze the target clot/plug upon direct contact (typically for 10 seconds). The frozen clot, probe, and bronchoscope are then withdrawn simultaneously as a single unit. - Safety Rule: To prevent the coolant from freezing fluid inside the working channel and rupturing the inner lining, the cryoprobe tip must extend at least 4 to 5 mm distal to the bronchoscope tip during freezing. The probe must be completely thawed (e.g., in warm water) before attempting to withdraw it back through the channel. - En Bloc ETT Removal: For massive clots that cannot fit through the ETT, the scope, clot, and ETT must be removed together as a unit. Clinicians must be prepared for immediate, rapid re-intubation.
section_pearls: Cryoextraction is highly effective (92-100% success) for clot/mucus plug removal; ensure the cryoprobe tip extends 4-5 mm distal to the bronchoscope tip during freezing.
For massive clots, be prepared for en bloc removal of scope, clot, and ETT, with immediate re-intubation.

sections.heading: Special Clinical Situations
sections.content: Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (OHCA): Early FB (within 24 hours of admission/ROSC) to clear aspirated material is associated with shorter mechanical ventilation and intubation periods. - Inhalation Burns: FB within the first 24 hours is diagnostic, allowing grading of injury severity and prognosis. Serial surveillance bronchoscopies are generally not recommended unless severe inhalation injury warrants therapeutic lavage. - Aspiration Pneumonitis: Early FB (within 24 hours) improves respiratory function, reduces the incidence of subsequent aspiration pneumonia, and guides targeted antibiotic therapy.
section_pearls: Early FB (within 24 hours) in OHCA and aspiration pneumonitis improves respiratory outcomes.
FB within 24 hours for inhalation burns is diagnostic, but serial surveillance is not routinely recommended.

strengths_limitations: This narrative review offers practical, bedside-focused guidance on FB in mechanically ventilated patients, detailing mechanical and physiological interactions. However, some recommendations are based on expert consensus and lung-model data rather than large-scale randomized controlled trials.
